content: We can frame the linguistic definition of reflection (as both light reflection and personal critical reflection from the high school English vocabulary unit) as a design principle for LLM self-reflection mechanisms: just as physical reflection bounces a light beam back to its origin while human reflexive reflection reinterprets a past thought, LLMs can be designed to bounce a generated solution back against the original problem's constraints to expose unaddressed gaps before revising outputs. This dual framing, which merges the physical and cognitive definitions of reflexion from Topic A, solves a key limitation of current single-perspective LLM self-reflection that only checks for logical consistency without grounding revisions back to the original problem's core requirements.
